title: Kubernetes + Open Liberty Learning Path
description: From zero to production-ready. A structured path to learn containers, Kubernetes, and Open Liberty deployment.

phases.title: Phase 1: Containers & Docker
phases.description: Understand containerization before jumping into orchestration. Docker is the foundation everything else builds on.

topics.title: Docker Fundamentals
topics.description: Images, containers, layers, registries. How a Dockerfile works.

topics.title: Write Your First Dockerfile
topics.description: Create a Dockerfile for a simple Java/Liberty app. Build it, run it, see it work.
steps: Install Docker Desktop
Create a basic Dockerfile using `FROM icr.io/appcafe/open-liberty:kernel-slim-java17-openj9-ubi`
Copy a simple `server.xml` and WAR file
Run `docker build -t my-liberty-app .`
Run `docker run -p 9080:9080 my-liberty-app`
Open http://localhost:9080 — you're running Liberty in a container

topics.title: Container Networking & Volumes
topics.description: How containers talk to each other. How data persists (or doesn't).

topics.title: Push to a Registry
topics.description: Tag your image and push it to Docker Hub or GitHub Container Registry.
steps: Create a free Docker Hub account (or use GitHub Container Registry)
Tag your image: `docker tag my-liberty-app yourusername/my-liberty-app:v1`
Push it: `docker push yourusername/my-liberty-app:v1`
Pull it on another machine or delete local and re-pull to verify

topics.title: Multi-stage Builds
topics.description: Build your app AND package it in one Dockerfile. Keeps images small and secure.

phases.checkpoint: You can containerize a Liberty app, run it locally, and push to a registry.

phases.title: Phase 2: Kubernetes Fundamentals
phases.description: Learn Kubernetes core concepts. This is where the VS Code Kubernetes extension becomes your best friend.

topics.title: Set Up a Local Cluster
topics.description: Get a K8s cluster running on your machine.
steps: Option A: Enable Kubernetes in Docker Desktop (Settings → Kubernetes → Enable)
Option B: Install Minikube (`brew install minikube` on Mac)
Install kubectl: `brew install kubectl`
Verify: `kubectl cluster-info` and `kubectl get nodes`
Install the VS Code Kubernetes extension — see your cluster in the sidebar

topics.title: Pods, Deployments, Services
topics.description: The three objects you'll use 90% of the time. Pod = container(s). Deployment = manages pods. Service = exposes pods.

topics.title: Deploy Your First App
topics.description: Write YAML manifests and deploy your Liberty app to K8s.
steps: Create `deployment.yaml` — define a Deployment with 2 replicas of your Liberty image
Create `service.yaml` — expose it with a NodePort or LoadBalancer
Apply: `kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml -f service.yaml`
Watch pods come up: `kubectl get pods -w` (or use VS Code extension)
Access your app via the Service URL
Scale it: `kubectl scale deployment liberty-app --replicas=3`
Watch the new pod spin up in VS Code's K8s tree

topics.title: Essential kubectl Commands
topics.description: The commands you'll use every day.

topics.title: Debugging a Failing Pod
topics.description: Intentionally break something, then fix it using K8s tools.
steps: Deploy with a wrong image name — watch it go `ImagePullBackOff`
Run `kubectl describe pod <name>` — read the Events section
Fix the image, re-apply, watch it recover
Check logs: `kubectl logs <pod-name>` or use VS Code log viewer
Exec into a running pod: `kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/bash`

topics.title: Namespaces & Context Switching
topics.description: Organize resources into namespaces. Switch between clusters/namespaces.

phases.checkpoint: You can deploy, scale, debug, and manage apps on a local K8s cluster.

phases.title: Phase 3: Open Liberty on Kubernetes
phases.description: Apply K8s concepts specifically to Open Liberty. This is where it gets practical for your team's work.

topics.title: Liberty Container Best Practices
topics.description: Official guidance on running Liberty in containers — image selection, features, configuration.

topics.title: Health Checks & Probes
topics.description: Wire up Liberty's MicroProfile Health endpoints to K8s readiness/liveness probes.
steps: Add `mpHealth-4.0` feature to server.xml
Create a custom health check class implementing `HealthCheck`
Add readiness probe to deployment YAML: `httpGet /health/ready port 9080`
Add liveness probe: `httpGet /health/live port 9080`
Deploy and verify: `kubectl describe pod` shows probe status
Kill the DB connection — watch K8s restart the pod automatically

topics.title: Externalize Configuration
topics.description: Use ConfigMaps and Secrets to manage server.xml and credentials without rebuilding images.
steps: Create a ConfigMap from your server.xml: `kubectl create configmap liberty-config --from-file=server.xml`
Mount it as a volume in your Deployment
Create a Secret for DB password: `kubectl create secret generic db-creds --from-literal=password=mypass`
Reference the Secret as an env var in your container spec
Use MicroProfile Config to read env vars in your Java code
Change the ConfigMap → restart pods → see config change without rebuilding

topics.title: Open Liberty Operator
topics.description: The operator automates Liberty deployments — handles routing, TLS, scaling, and day-2 operations.

topics.title: Rolling Updates & Zero-Downtime Deploys
topics.description: Update your app version without dropping requests.
steps: Build v2 of your Liberty app with a visible change
Push to registry with new tag
Update Deployment image tag
Watch the rolling update: old pods terminate as new ones become ready
Verify with `kubectl rollout status deployment/liberty-app`
Rollback if needed: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/liberty-app`

phases.checkpoint: You can deploy Liberty with proper health checks, externalized config, and zero-downtime updates.

phases.title: Phase 4: Production Patterns
phases.description: The patterns that separate a demo from a production deployment. Resource management, autoscaling, observability.

topics.title: Resource Limits & Requests
topics.description: Tell K8s how much CPU and memory your Liberty app needs. Prevents noisy neighbors and OOM kills.

topics.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
topics.description: Auto-scale your Liberty deployment based on CPU usage.
steps: Install metrics-server (if using Minikube: `minikube addons enable metrics-server`)
Set resource requests in your Deployment (e.g., `cpu: 200m`)
Create HPA: `kubectl autoscale deployment liberty-app --cpu-percent=50 --min=2 --max=10`
Generate load (use `hey` or `ab` tool)
Watch pods scale up: `kubectl get hpa -w`
Stop load — watch it scale back down

topics.title: Ingress & TLS
topics.description: Route external traffic to your services with proper HTTPS.

topics.title: Helm Charts
topics.description: Package your entire Liberty deployment as a reusable Helm chart.
steps: Install Helm: `brew install helm`
Create a chart: `helm create liberty-app`
Customize templates for your Liberty deployment, service, configmap
Add values.yaml for environment-specific config (dev/staging/prod)
Install: `helm install my-app ./liberty-app`
Upgrade: `helm upgrade my-app ./liberty-app --set image.tag=v2`

topics.title: Observability: Logs, Metrics, Traces
topics.description: Monitor your Liberty apps in K8s — MicroProfile Metrics, JSON logging, distributed tracing.

topics.title: CI/CD Pipeline
topics.description: Automate build → test → deploy with GitHub Actions or Jenkins.

phases.checkpoint: You can run Liberty in production with autoscaling, monitoring, TLS, and automated deployments.

tools.name: VS Code Kubernetes Extension
tools.description: Cluster browser, logs, exec, YAML intellisense

tools.name: VS Code Liberty Tools
tools.description: Dev mode, hot reload, run/debug Liberty

tools.name: Minikube
tools.description: Local K8s cluster (alternative to Docker Desktop)

tools.name: KillerCoda
tools.description: Free browser-based K8s labs — no local setup needed

books.title: Kubernetes in Action
books.author: Marko Lukša
books.description: The best comprehensive K8s book. Covers everything from pods to operators.

books.title: The Kubernetes Book
books.author: Nigel Poulton
books.description: Shorter, more accessible intro. Great for getting started fast.

books.title: Cloud Native Java
books.author: Josh Long & Kenny Bastani
books.description: Java-specific cloud-native patterns including K8s deployment.
